assertions.name: one_question_at_a_time
assertions.description: Asks exactly one question per turn, waits for user response before asking the next.

rubric.0: Asks 3 or more questions in a single turn, or fires a rapid-sequence list of questions without waiting for any answer
rubric.25: Asks 2 questions in a single turn, making the user decide which to answer first
rubric.50: Asks one question but immediately hedges or appends a follow-up in the same message that forces a second answer
rubric.75: Asks one question per turn consistently with at most one minor slip that does not derail the flow
rubric.100: Every turn contains exactly one focused question. Waits for the user's answer before moving on. No bundled follow-ups.

assertions.name: recommend_with_each_question
assertions.description: Each question is accompanied by the skill's recommended answer, not just an open-ended prompt.

rubric.0: No recommendations given at all — purely asks questions and waits
rubric.25: Occasionally provides a recommendation but skips it most of the time
rubric.50: Provides recommendations roughly half the time; inconsistent — some questions get a recommendation, others don't
rubric.75: Provides a recommendation with most questions; one or two questions are left open-ended without a clear reason
rubric.100: Every question includes a concrete recommended answer. The recommendation is stated before or alongside the question, not buried after.

assertions.name: codebase_exploration_over_asking
assertions.description: When a question can be answered by exploring the codebase, explores instead of asking the user.

rubric.0: Never explores codebase; asks user questions that could be resolved by reading the code
rubric.25: Explores only when explicitly prompted by the user; misses obvious opportunities to self-answer
rubric.50: Explores occasionally but inconsistently; sometimes asks questions that a grep or file read would resolve
rubric.75: Explores the codebase proactively in most cases; at most one missed opportunity where asking replaces reading
rubric.100: Always explores the codebase when the answer is findable there. Only asks the user when the answer requires human judgment or intent.

assertions.name: glossary_conflict_detection
assertions.description: Immediately calls out when user uses a term that conflicts with existing CONTEXT.md definitions.

rubric.0: Ignores glossary conflicts entirely; lets contradictory terminology pass without comment
rubric.25: Notices a conflict but addresses it much later in the session rather than immediately
rubric.50: Calls out the conflict but frames it vaguely without quoting the existing definition or naming the tension precisely
rubric.75: Calls out the conflict immediately in most cases; quotes the glossary definition; one miss or minor delay
rubric.100: Every terminology conflict is surfaced immediately. Quotes the existing CONTEXT.md definition. Names exactly what differs. Asks the user to resolve it before continuing.

assertions.name: sharpen_fuzzy_language
assertions.description: Proposes a precise canonical term when the user uses vague or overloaded language.

rubric.0: Adopts the user's vague language uncritically; never proposes sharper terms
rubric.25: Occasionally notes that a term is fuzzy but does not propose a replacement
rubric.50: Proposes sharper terms sometimes; misses obvious overloaded terms that should be disambiguated
rubric.75: Consistently proposes precise alternatives for vague terms; at most one missed opportunity
rubric.100: Every overloaded or vague term is caught and a precise canonical alternative is proposed. The proposal names the ambiguity and offers a resolution.

assertions.name: concrete_scenario_stress_test
assertions.description: Invents specific edge-case scenarios to probe domain relationship boundaries when those relationships are being discussed.

rubric.0: Discusses domain relationships only abstractly; never invents a concrete scenario to stress-test boundaries
rubric.25: Uses a scenario only when the user explicitly requests an example; does not proactively stress-test
rubric.50: Invents scenarios occasionally but they are generic rather than targeted at the specific edge case in question
rubric.75: Invents targeted scenarios for most domain relationship discussions; at most one missed opportunity
rubric.100: Proactively invents specific, targeted edge-case scenarios whenever domain relationships are discussed. Each scenario forces precision about concept boundaries.

assertions.name: cross_reference_with_code
assertions.description: When user states how something works, checks the code and surfaces any contradiction.

rubric.0: Never checks the code against user claims; accepts all statements at face value
rubric.25: Checks code only when the user explicitly requests it; misses unsolicited contradictions
rubric.50: Checks code sometimes but inconsistently; surfaces only obvious contradictions while missing subtler ones
rubric.75: Proactively checks code for most user claims; surfaces contradictions clearly; at most one missed check
rubric.100: Every factual claim about how the system works is verified against the codebase. Any contradiction is surfaced immediately with the specific file/line evidence.

assertions.name: context_md_inline_update
assertions.description: Updates CONTEXT.md immediately when a term is resolved, without batching updates for later.

rubric.0: Never updates CONTEXT.md, or batches all updates to the end of the session
rubric.25: Updates CONTEXT.md but only after several terms have accumulated, not inline
rubric.50: Updates inline sometimes; batches other updates; inconsistent timing
rubric.75: Updates CONTEXT.md immediately in most cases; at most one batched update that should have been inline
rubric.100: Every resolved term triggers an immediate CONTEXT.md update. No batching. Creates CONTEXT.md if it does not exist.

assertions.name: adr_sparingly
assertions.description: Only offers to create an ADR when all three criteria are met: hard-to-reverse, surprising without context, and real trade-off.

rubric.0: Offers ADRs for routine decisions that are easy to reverse, unsurprising, or involve no real trade-off
rubric.25: Offers ADRs inconsistently — sometimes for trivial decisions, sometimes misses genuinely ADR-worthy ones
rubric.50: ADR offers are reasonable but skill fails to check all three criteria — e.g. offers ADR for a decision that meets two criteria but not the third
rubric.75: ADR offers are generally well-targeted; at most one borderline offer or one missed opportunity
rubric.100: Offers ADR only when all three criteria are demonstrably met. Skips ADR when any criterion is missing. When offering, names which criteria are satisfied.

assertions.name: session_end_protocol
assertions.description: Signals completion with exact phrase, then asks exactly one AskUserQuestion with the specified options.

rubric.0: Does not signal completion at all, or ends without any structured question, or implements code/creates files without authorization
rubric.25: Signals completion but skips the AskUserQuestion, or asks a free-text question instead of structured options
rubric.50: Signals completion and asks a question but omits required options or adds unauthorized options
rubric.75: Signals completion with correct phrase and asks AskUserQuestion with correct options; minor phrasing deviation only
rubric.100: Signals end with exact phrase 'Grill session complete. All branches resolved.' Then asks exactly one AskUserQuestion with options: Create a seed, Nothing yet. No other actions taken.

assertions.name: no_implementation_without_auth
assertions.description: Does not implement code or create files (other than CONTEXT.md and ADR updates) without explicit post-session user authorization.

rubric.0: Creates code files, modifies source, or takes implementation actions during or after the session without authorization
rubric.25: Proposes implementation steps as if ready to execute them, blurring the line between planning and doing
rubric.50: Stays within bounds during session but ambiguously frames next steps in a way that implies imminent action without waiting for authorization
rubric.75: Correctly withholds implementation throughout; minor phrasing suggests eagerness but no actual unauthorized action
rubric.100: Zero unauthorized implementation. CONTEXT.md and ADR files are the only files touched during session. Post-session, waits fully for explicit authorization before any action.

assertions.name: relentless_branch_coverage
assertions.description: Walks every branch of the design tree and resolves dependencies between decisions one by one before moving on.

rubric.0: Skips entire decision branches or moves on before resolving open questions; leaves major dependencies unaddressed
rubric.25: Covers main path only; skips edge cases or sub-branches when they become apparent
rubric.50: Covers most branches but leaves one significant unresolved dependency or skips a visible sub-branch
rubric.75: Thorough coverage; resolves dependencies in order; at most one minor branch left slightly under-explored
rubric.100: Every branch of the design tree is walked. Dependencies are resolved in the correct order before downstream decisions are made. No open question is abandoned.

assertions.name: number_consistency
assertions.description: All counts, quantities, list lengths, and numeric references are internally consistent. If the output says '5 items' then exactly 5 items appear. Step numbers are sequential with no gaps or duplicates.

rubric.0: Multiple numeric contradictions: stated counts don't match actual counts, step numbers skip or repeat, quantities referenced earlier differ from later usage
rubric.25: One clear numeric mismatch: a stated count disagrees with actual items, or a step number is wrong
rubric.50: Numbers are technically correct but ambiguous: vague quantities like 'several' alongside exact counts, or implicit counts that require reader inference to verify
rubric.75: All numbers consistent with at most one trivially unclear reference that does not mislead
rubric.100: Every count, step number, list length, and numeric reference is exact and verifiable against the output content

assertions.name: no_contradictions
assertions.description: No statement in the output contradicts another statement in the same output. Instructions do not conflict. Constraints stated in one section are not violated in another.

rubric.0: Direct contradictions: output says X in one place and not-X in another, or gives conflicting instructions for the same action
rubric.25: One clear contradiction or two statements that are difficult to reconcile without assuming one is wrong
rubric.50: No direct contradictions but some tension between sections that could confuse a reader about intended behavior
rubric.75: Fully consistent with at most one minor ambiguity that does not create a real conflict
rubric.100: Zero contradictions. Every statement is compatible with every other statement. Constraints are honored throughout

assertions.name: plan_cohesion
assertions.description: The output presents a cohesive path from start to finish. Steps build on each other logically. The roadmap, plan, or sequence has no orphaned steps, circular dependencies, or gaps where the reader must guess how to get from A to B.

rubric.0: Disjointed output: steps reference things not yet introduced, sections appear unrelated, or the overall flow has no clear direction
rubric.25: General direction exists but significant gaps: a step depends on something never mentioned, or sections are loosely connected with missing transitions
rubric.50: Flow is followable but has one structural gap or an orphaned section that does not connect to the rest
rubric.75: Cohesive flow with clear progression. At most one minor ordering issue that does not block understanding
rubric.100: Fully cohesive: each section builds on prior context, dependencies are introduced before use, and the output reads as a unified whole

scenarios.name: simple_feature_plan_no_context
scenarios.prompt: I want to add a notification system to our app. Users should get emails when their report is ready, and we might want to add in-app notifications later. There's no existing notification infrastructure.
scenarios.context: Small SaaS app, no CONTEXT.md exists yet, no docs/adr/ directory. Codebase has a reports/ module and a users/ module with basic CRUD.

scenarios.name: glossary_conflict_session
scenarios.prompt: I want to redesign how we handle accounts. Each account should be able to have multiple workspaces, and users can belong to multiple accounts.
scenarios.context: CONTEXT.md exists and defines: 'Account: the billing entity owned by an Organization. An Organization has exactly one Account.' The user's plan uses 'account' to mean what the glossary calls 'Organization'.

scenarios.name: vague_terminology_session
scenarios.prompt: We need to let accounts share data with each other. An account can grant access to another account, and the receiving account can read but not write. We also need account-level audit logs.
scenarios.context: No CONTEXT.md. The codebase has both a Tenant model (billing/multi-tenancy) and a UserProfile model (individual user identity). The user's 'account' could refer to either.

scenarios.name: code_contradiction_session
scenarios.prompt: Our order system supports partial cancellations — a user can cancel individual line items without cancelling the whole order. I want to add a refund flow that triggers when any item is cancelled.
scenarios.context: CONTEXT.md defines 'Cancellation: voids the entire Order. Partial line-item removal is called Removal, not Cancellation.' The codebase Order model has a cancel() method that sets status='cancelled' on the whole order, with no partial-cancel logic.

scenarios.name: adr_worthy_decision
scenarios.prompt: I'm planning to move our job queue from a Postgres-backed queue to Redis Streams. The main reason is throughput — we're hitting lock contention at high volume. I think we should just do it.
scenarios.context: Existing CONTEXT.md documents the job queue as 'JobQueue: durable task execution backed by Postgres for transactional consistency with writes.' The codebase uses pg-boss. No ADR exists for the original Postgres decision.
